name: Aluminium
name_origin: Derived from the Latin 'alumen' (meaning alum), a sulfate mineral known since antiquity. The IUPAC name is 'aluminium'; 'aluminum' is a common variant in American English.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Aluminium is the most abundant metal and the third most abundant element in Earth's crust (~8.1%). It is too reactive to occur as the free metal and is mined primarily as bauxite ore.

compounds.name: Aluminium oxide
compounds.summary: Alumina; an extremely hard ceramic used as an abrasive, refractory, and the precursor for smelting aluminium metal. Gemstone forms include sapphire and ruby.

compounds.name: Aluminium hydroxide
compounds.summary: Used as an antacid, a flame retardant, and an intermediate in producing alumina from bauxite (Bayer process).

compounds.name: Aluminium chloride
compounds.summary: A strong Lewis acid catalyst used in Friedel-Crafts reactions in organic synthesis.

compounds.name: Potassium alum
compounds.summary: Used as a mordant for dyes, in water purification, and as a styptic.

compounds.name: Cryolite
compounds.summary: Used as a flux in the Hall-Heroult electrolytic smelting of aluminium.

compounds.name: Aluminium sulfate
compounds.summary: A flocculant in water and wastewater treatment and used in papermaking.

uses: Lightweight structural metal in aircraft, vehicles, and high-speed trains
Beverage cans, food packaging, and household foil
Power transmission lines (cheaper and lighter than copper for the same conductance)
Building materials: window frames, siding, roofing, and curtain walls
Aluminium alloys in consumer electronics and cookware
Thermite reactions for welding rail tracks and incendiary devices
reactivity_summary: Aluminium is a reactive metal but rapidly forms a thin, tightly adherent oxide layer in air that protects the underlying metal from further corrosion. It dissolves in both strong acids and strong bases (amphoteric) and reacts vigorously with halogens.
isotopes_summary: Aluminium has only one stable isotope, Al-27. The most notable radioisotope is Al-26 with a half-life of about 717,000 years, used in geological dating and as a tracer in cosmochemistry.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Aluminium
